title: Demonstration of Exponential Quantum Speedup with Constant-Depth Compiled Circuits for Simon's Problem
authors: Phattharaporn Singkanipa, Victor Kasatkin, Daniel A. Lidar
abstract: We demonstrate exponential quantum speedup for a restricted-Hamming-weight version of Simon's problem on present-day superconducting quantum processors by introducing a hardware-aware compilation strategy that compiles the quantum part of each Simon query circuit to constant depth. The resulting compiled circuits have $O(1)$ depth and linear connectivity, map directly onto common device layouts, and avoid additional routing and SWAP overhead. Implemented on IBM's $156$-qubit Boston and $120$-qubit Miami processors, the resulting circuits achieve sufficiently high fidelity to exhibit algorithmic quantum speedup without error suppression. Using the number-of-queries-to-solution metric, we observe exponential speedup over the classical lower bound across the full Hamming-weight range studied on Boston and across low-to-intermediate Hamming weights on Miami; at higher Hamming weights on Miami, we still observe polynomial speedup. The same construction also reaches a regime where the original Simon problem is recovered for the problem sizes studied. These results show that careful hardware-aware compilation can make exponential quantum speedup experimentally accessible for a canonical hidden-subgroup problem in the NISQ regime.
summary: This paper presents a method to implement Simon's algorithm with constant-depth circuits on NISQ hardware by using a hardware-aware compilation strategy. By reducing circuit depth, the authors successfully demonstrated exponential quantum speedup on large-scale superconducting processors.
main_problem: Demonstrating exponential quantum speedup for a version of Simon's problem on NISQ-era superconducting hardware while overcoming high circuit depth and SWAP overhead.
main_result: The authors achieved exponential speedup on the 156-qubit 'Boston' processor and both exponential and polynomial speedup on the 120-qubit 'Miami' processor using a constant-depth compilation strategy.
method: A hardware-aware compilation strategy that reduces the quantum part of Simon queries to $O(1)$ depth and a Bayesian post-processing algorithm to identify the hidden bitstring.
model_or_system: Superconducting quantum processors from IBM, specifically the 15FR-qubit 'Boston' (heavy-hex connectivity) and 120-qubit 'Miami' (square-lattice connectivity) devices.
key_observables: Number-of-queries-to-solution (NTS), NTS for ideal quantum (NTS_IQ), and scaling exponents (alpha for polylog, beta for poly).
important_parameters: Hamming weight (w) of the hidden bitstring, problem size (n), and the use of the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) for model selection.
main_assumptions: The speedup is demonstrated for a restricted-Hamming-weight version of Simon's problem; comparison is made against a theoretical classical lower bound rather than an optimal classical player.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the quantum circuit construction, the comparison of oracle methods, scaling plots of NTS vs. problem size/Hamming weight, and statistical diagnostics (AIC/R2) for model comparison.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem and circuit construction, details the hardware-aware compilation method, presents experimental results on two IBM processors, performs statistical scaling analysis to distinguish between exponential and polynomial speedup, and discusses the impact of error mitigation tools.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Comparison of two oracle constructions for Simon-5 (b = 01111) in the graph representation. CNOTs can be placed in the same entangling layer when they do not share any qubits. For the HW(b) = 4 instance shown here, the construction presented in this work can be scheduled in two entangling layers, whereas the con- struction in Ref. [47] requires three under the same non-overlapping- qubit scheduling rule.

FIG. 3. Circuit construction underlying the compiled Simon-5 query circuits in the graph representation. Using the reduction procedure, we can trace out an appropriate subset of the leading dashed boxes to obtain Simon-m for any m satisfying HW(b) ≤m < 5.
FIG. 4. Circuit for w2Simon-3 compiled on Boston using the ALAP scheduling strategy. The native gates shown are Rz (circular arrows), √

FIG. 5. log2(NTSQ) as a function of n (bottom axis) for the restricted-HW Simon problem with cutoff w = 3 on Boston (blue, up to n = 63) and Miami (red, up to n = 60); the top axis shows log2(Nw). The solid lines denote fits using the polylog model [Eq. (8)], and the dashed lines denote fits using the poly model [Eq. (9)]. The error bars show bootstrapped 1σ uncertainties. The yellow dash-dot lines represent the scaling of log2(NTSlb C ) and also serve as a visual guide for the large-n scaling of the poly model. The gray dashed line is log2 of the interpolated noiseless-quantum esti- mate given by Eq. (6).

FIG. 6. log2(NTSQ) as a function of n on Boston (blue) and Miami (red) for the original wnSimon-n, up to n = 20 and n = 16, respec- tively. The line styles and reference curves are the same as in Fig. 5.
